terms.def: Sensitivity of an asset's returns to the market; β=1 moves with the market, β=2 moves twice as much.
E[Rm] − r_f: the extra return demanded for holding the market portfolio over risk-free assets; historically ~5–6%.
Return above the CAPM prediction; positive α means the asset earned more than its beta justified.
Security Market Line — plots expected return vs beta; assets on the SML are fairly priced; above = positive α.
Market risk that cannot be diversified away; CAPM says only systematic risk is compensated by higher expected return.
Return on a zero-risk asset (typically 3-month T-bill); the baseline against which risk premia are measured.

eli3: CAPM (Capital Asset Pricing Model) says that the expected return of any investment depends on just one thing: how much the investment moves with the overall market. An investment that closely follows the market (high beta) should have a higher expected return because it's riskier. An investment that barely moves with the market (low beta) should have a lower expected return.

The idea: if two investments have the same expected return, rational investors prefer the one that moves less with the market — it provides better insurance when the market crashes. For investors to hold the riskier investment, it must offer higher expected return as compensation.

CAPM was derived independently by Sharpe (1964), Lintner (1965), and Mossin (1966), building on Markowitz's portfolio theory. It says every stock's expected excess return = beta × market risk premium. The market risk premium is the extra return investors demand for holding stocks instead of risk-free assets.

whenWhy.title: Estimating the cost of equity capital
whenWhy.body: The most common application of CAPM in corporate finance: estimating the required return on equity capital for capital budgeting and valuation. CAPM formula: E[R_i] = r_f + β_i × (E[R_m] - r_f). Inputs: r_f (current 10-year Treasury yield, typically 4-5% in 2024), β_i (estimated from historical regression of stock returns on market returns, often from a data provider), E[R_m] - r_f (equity risk premium, typically estimated at 5-7% using historical data or implied from market prices). The resulting cost of equity is used as the discount rate for free cash flow projections in DCF valuation. Despite its empirical limitations, CAPM remains the most widely used cost-of-equity model in practice due to its simplicity.

whenWhy.title: Measuring active manager performance (Jensen's alpha)
whenWhy.body: Jensen's alpha: α = R_i - [r_f + β_i(R_m - r_f)]. The intercept from regressing excess returns on market excess returns. A positive alpha indicates the manager earned more than CAPM predicts given their market exposure — evidence of genuine skill or factor exposure. Alpha should be estimated with: (1) sufficient time series (5+ years); (2) appropriate benchmark (not just the S&P 500 for a small-cap manager — use a small-cap index beta); (3) standard errors — most alphas are statistically indistinguishable from zero, especially over short periods. The extension to multi-factor alphas (Fama-French, Carhart) is more rigorous — many apparent CAPM alphas disappear when controlling for size, value, and momentum factors.

whenWhy.title: Security Market Line and portfolio construction
whenWhy.body: The Security Market Line (SML) is the CAPM equilibrium: E[R_i] = r_f + β_i × ERP. Every security plots on the SML in equilibrium. Securities above the SML are underpriced (expected return > CAPM prediction → buy signal). Securities below the SML are overpriced (expected return < CAPM prediction → sell signal). In practice, identifying securities persistently above the SML (positive alpha) is the goal of active management — and one of the most empirically challenging tasks in finance. The SML provides the theoretical benchmark against which active managers' stock selection is evaluated.

deepDiveIntro: CAPM is derived by assuming all investors hold the same efficient portfolio of risky assets and can borrow or lend at the risk-free rate. In equilibrium, that shared portfolio must be the market portfolio. The expected return of any individual asset above the risk-free rate equals beta times the market risk premium, where beta measures how much the asset contributes to the overall market portfolio's variance — the only risk investors cannot eliminate through diversification.

A stock's total return variance splits into two parts: systematic variance (driven by broad market moves, captured by beta) and idiosyncratic variance (driven by company-specific events). In a diversified portfolio, idiosyncratic variance cancels out across holdings. CAPM's core claim is that only systematic risk is rewarded — investors should not expect extra return simply for taking on diversifiable risk.

deepDive.heading: Beta Estimation and Its Pitfalls
deepDive.body: Beta is estimated by regressing a stock's excess returns on the market's excess returns over a historical window, typically 3–5 years. Common pitfalls include beta instability over time (a bank's beta can surge during a financial crisis), thin-trading bias for illiquid stocks (their prices don't react immediately to market moves, understating the true co-movement), and the tendency of estimated betas to mean-revert toward 1.0. Blume's adjustment corrects for this by blending the historical beta two-thirds with 1.0 one-third. [1]

deepDive.heading: CAPM Empirical Tests and Anomalies
deepDive.body: Decades of empirical tests show that the actual relationship between beta and returns is much flatter than CAPM predicts — high-beta stocks earn less than they should, and low-beta stocks earn more. Beyond this beta anomaly, researchers found that small-cap stocks, high book-to-market (value) stocks, and recent winners all systematically outperform in ways that beta alone cannot explain. These findings motivated multi-factor models like Fama-French (which adds size and value factors) and Carhart (which adds momentum) as more empirically accurate alternatives. [2]

deepDive.heading: CAPM in Practice: Cost of Capital
deepDive.body: CAPM is the dominant tool for estimating a company's cost of equity in corporate finance: cost of equity equals the risk-free rate plus beta times the equity risk premium. Using a risk-free rate of 4.5%, an equity risk premium of 5.5%, and a beta of 1.2 gives a cost of equity of 11.1%. This feeds into the weighted average cost of capital (WACC) used in discounted cash flow valuations. Survey evidence shows that around 74% of CFOs use CAPM for this purpose despite its empirical limitations. When valuing investments in emerging markets, a country risk premium is added on top. [1]

takeaways: CAPM: E[R_i] = r_f + β_i × (E[R_m] - r_f). Only systematic risk (beta) earns a return premium. [1]
Beta = Cov(R_i, R_m) / Var(R_m) — measures contribution to market portfolio variance. [1]
Jensen's alpha: intercept of excess return regression — positive alpha indicates risk-adjusted outperformance. [1]
CAPM fails empirically: cross-sectional beta-return relationship is too flat; size, value, momentum effects are unexplained. [2]
74% of CFOs use CAPM for cost of equity — dominant in practice despite theoretical limitations. [1]

edgeCases.q: If CAPM fails empirically, why is it still widely used?
edgeCases.a: CAPM persists for several reasons: (1) Simplicity — it produces a single, easily interpretable number (beta) for the risk-return trade-off; multi-factor models require estimating multiple betas and factor premia; (2) Consensus — cost-of-capital estimates need to be defensible to shareholders, regulators, and counterparties; CAPM's widespread use makes its estimates the 'industry standard'; (3) No obviously superior alternative — multi-factor models have better empirical fit but which factors to use is contested (Fama-French 3 vs. 5 vs. 6-factor; Carhart 4-factor?); (4) CAPM is directionally correct — more systematic risk should command higher return; CAPM's failure is quantitative, not directional; (5) Good enough for long-horizon valuation — the errors in CAPM beta estimation are small relative to uncertainty in growth rate projections in a DCF; optimising the cost of equity model is second-order. The pragmatic view: use CAPM as the baseline; apply multi-factor adjustments for portfolio construction and performance evaluation where empirical precision matters more.

edgeCases.q: How does CAPM handle assets with negative beta?
edgeCases.a: CAPM predicts that assets with negative beta should have expected returns BELOW the risk-free rate: E[R_i] = r_f + β_i × ERP; if β_i < 0 and ERP > 0, then E[R_i] < r_f. This is rational: a negative-beta asset provides insurance — it pays off when the market falls, exactly when the insurance is most valuable. Investors are willing to accept below-risk-free expected returns for this insurance property. Real-world examples: (1) Long Treasury bonds in normal conditions (β ≈ -0.1 to -0.3 vs. equity market — flight-to-safety reduces bond yields when equities fall); (2) Long volatility positions (VIX calls): strongly negative beta — pay off in equity crashes; (3) Gold: sometimes negative beta; (4) Trend-following strategies: empirically negative beta in equity crashes (crisis alpha). In practice, truly negative beta assets are rare and often have zero or near-zero beta. The CAPM prediction of below-risk-free expected returns for negative beta assets is empirically verified for gold and long bonds in certain environments.

sources.label: Sharpe, W.F. (1964) — Capital Asset Prices: A Theory of Market Equilibrium Under Conditions of Risk, Journal of Finance
Fama, E. & French, K. (1992) — The Cross-Section of Expected Stock Returns, Journal of Finance
